chunks.title: Introduction to Blood Glucose and Diabetes
chunks.summary: The discussion opens with a consideration of diabetes, questioning whether it is solely driven by blood glucose levels. It references the historical Kempner study where individuals lost weight and managed diabetes on a white rice and sugar diet, leading to further inquiries about the true causes of diabetes.

chunks.title: Diabetes and Drug Mechanisms
chunks.summary: The speaker talks about the approved drug ACP MOX and its effects on type 2 diabetes, focusing on how it reduces triglycerides and blood glucose despite the presence of obesity. This leads to an explanation of the Randle cycle and how glucose and fat compete for energy metabolism.

chunks.title: Inhibition of Glucose Metabolism
chunks.summary: Further discussion revolves around how an excess of fatty acids inhibits glucose metabolism, referencing the role of pyruvate in energy production. The speaker explains how fatty acid oxidation can disrupt the enzyme responsible for metabolizing glucose, leading to inadequate energy utilization.

chunks.title: Effects of Fat on Diabetes and Insulin Resistance
chunks.summary: The segment continues exploring how the overabundance of fats correlates with insulin resistance and diabetes. The speaker discusses studies showing the effects of high-fat diets on fat accumulation and the resulting metabolic dysfunction.

chunks.title: Aspirin and Diabetes Management
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the therapeutic potential of aspirin in managing diabetes, discussing how high doses have restored insulin sensitivity in morbidly obese diabetes patients. The anti-inflammatory properties of aspirin and their effect on cortisol levels are highlighted.

chunks.title: Evidence Supporting Dietary Changes
chunks.summary: The importance of dietary interventions in treating diabetes is emphasized, focusing on the role of carbohydrates versus fats. Historical references are made to studies where dietary sugars were used therapeutically in managing diabetes.

chunks.title: Conclusion: Linking Fatty Acids to Insulin Resistance
chunks.summary: The segment concludes by tying together the discussion around free fatty acids and their relationship to insulin resistance. The speaker reiterates that insulin resistance is influenced by excess fatty acids in the bloodstream, fueling the discussion around effective dietary strategies for diabetes management.
chunks.transcript: gallons of it daily in Sweden and these people were fine even type 1 diabetics so that was that was amazing thank you I know that was kind of technical for a lot of the listeners I'll just try and summarize it and basically we can backstep to where we're actually getting close to the roots of diabetes but the idea is that excess lipolysis excess free fatty acids in the blood trigger the periphery whether it's the muscles the liver the brain to become insulin resistant we know this we know I mean one of the things that we can even measure in humans is what is the the nifa the non-sterified fatty acids in the blood what are the free fatty acids and when you're doing ketosis when you're fasting if you're limiting carbohydrates you will see the free fatty acids go up and that's a physiologic insulin resistance response it's not the same as a pathologic insulin resistance but it is going to raise cortisol and that as Georgie talked about on previous podcasts and a little bit on this one when you're fasting when you're doing ketosis you will raise 11 beta hydroxy steroid dehydrogenase which is going to lead to excess cortisol and so you're you are triggering these sort of stress mechanisms when you limit carbohydrates But ultimately it's the excess free fatty acids in the blood that cause the periphery to be resistant to insulin that is insulin
